## [00:00 - 03:01]

Okay, so earlier I talked about the perceived value and a lot of times the perceived value, well it's something you have to really focus on and you have to focus on maintaining the perceived value of your information. If like for one way you can kill your perceived value is like if you consistently start having sales on your product like all of a sudden it's a 50% off sale like every month, you just killed your perceived value. Now you just, if you're selling for something for $47 and then once a month you put it on sale for $27, the people aren't stupid. They're watching what you're doing, they realize that it goes on sale all the time. And basically what you've done if you've changed your price from $47 to $27 because people are just going to wait until you drop that price again, you've killed your perceived value. So you have to be very, very careful in what you do that you maintain your perceived value. Now another thing is a lot of times changing the format of your product changes the perceived value and changes the real value. Like for instance one of the things I like to do is whitewater, like whitewater kayaking and whitewater rafting. I've actually done some crazy stuff, you know I've kayaked off waterfalls and crazy stuff like that and just one of the things I like to do. Now when you're out there running these rivers and going down these rapids, sometimes things go wrong. And so there's a whole art in science to what they call river rescue and that's putting together a rescue and it could be rescuing a boat or rescuing some gear or rescuing a person in the river. And so there's one book out there, so it's just a standard book, you can get it at Amazon and you know it's a book and I think it's called River Rescue. And I think it sells for I don't know somewhere, let's just say 20 bucks, between 10 and 20 dollars, it's just a standard book. Okay, that book sort of is the Bible of how you rescue, you conduct yourself in rescue situations. How do you save people? How do you save gear? How do you save boats? What do you do? And it's some pretty technical complicated stuff and then you can also, there's similar information and it's similar information could be you know in a workshop. And I have the book, I own the book, I've also taken the workshop where you go on the river and they teach you what to do on the river. This workshop I don't know, it could cost 200 to 500 dollars. Same basic information, same like you're getting the same information but here you're actually getting to practice it and you've got instructors that are working with you. So same information, delivered in a different format and this is you know way way more expensive

## [03:01 - 06:05]

but the reality is this is complicated stuff and being able to practice it, you know this is a lot more valuable. If the person's willing to take the time out to attend this training which could be a couple day training, this is super valuable. Now there's another format that I've seen recently and these are like some like laminated cards and they're you know so big. So in real life they're probably about like that big or maybe a little bit bigger and there's actually there's a ring and there's like 10 or 15 cards that are out like on this ring and now you go from this book is sort of big and bulky and if you're in a kayak there's not much room, there's almost no room in a kayak but in a kayak you can fit these cards in there and you can just wedge them in there inside your kayak. So now if you are in a situation where you have to rescue a boat or a gear or heaven forbid a person, now you can actually pull out the cards and like some of this stuff's complicated like there's this thing called the Z-Drag and that's where you hook up all these lines so that you have this mechanical advantage so then you can pull and get a you know a 9 to 1 mechanical advantage. I know I'm getting too technical here but the Z-Drag's a complicated thing to set up and unless you really know what you're doing and you've practiced at it a bunch and you've maintained your practice you can't remember how to do it. So like if you just read the book you're probably not going to remember. If you've done the workshop recently you're probably going to remember but if it's been a year or two since you did the workshop you're probably not going to remember and all of a sudden these cards well tell you the truth I can't remember what the cards sell for. They probably sell for 20 bucks but if you're in a situation where you're trying to rescue someone and they're in a life threatening situation the real value forget the perceived value these cards become priceless. So this is just an example of how the same information can move in all kinds of different formats and the perceived value can be very very different depending on your situation. Now in the next video I'm going to really to show you there's one other word that I'm going to introduce and that word is fungible. It's sort of a funny word but that's basically in the information marketing context the idea of information being fungible is you can take that information and you can sort of mold it and reshape it into different formats and it will have different perceived value and it will have different appeal. Some people they want this they want the workshop they don't want to read the book other people they don't want to take two days to go to the workshop so they just want the book and other people they want to make sure when they're out there in their boat on the river they're going to have these cards. So the information itself is fungible through different formats and depending on what you do with the information it can radically change your results and actually your clients and customers results as well. So in the next video I'm going to walk through an example of one of my clients one of my

## [06:05 - 06:17]

product launch formula clients and how he basically changed the format of his information same information but he changed the format and had radically radically different results.
